elements.sent-2411-003: Randomized experiments have been the gold standard for drawing causal inference.
elements.sent-2411-004: The conventional model-based approach has been one of the most popular ways for analyzing treatment effects from randomized experiments, which is often carried through inference for certain model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we provide a systematic investigation of model-based analyses for treatment effects under the randomization-based inference framework.
elements.sent-2411-006: This framework does not impose any distributional assumptions on the outcomes, covariates and their dependence, and utilizes only randomization as the ``reasoned basis''.
elements.sent-2411-007: We first derive the asymptotic theory for Z-estimation in completely randomized experiments, and propose sandwich-type conservative covariance estimation.
elements.sent-2411-008: We then apply the developed theory to analyze both average and individual treatment effects in randomized experiments.
elements.sent-2411-009: For the average treatment effect, we consider three estimation strategies: model-based, model-imputed, and model-assisted, where the first two can be sensitive to model misspecification or require specific ways for parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-010: The model-assisted approach is robust to arbitrary model misspecification and always provides consistent average treatment effect estimation.
elements.sent-2411-011: We propose optimal ways to conduct model-assisted estimation using generally nonlinear least squares for parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-012: For the individual treatment effects, we propose to directly model the relationship between individual effects and covariates, and discuss the model's identifiability, inference and interpretation allowing model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-013: Since \citet{fisher1925statistical , randomized experiments have become the ``gold standard'' for drawing causal inference.}
elements.sent-2411-014: There are at least two popular inferential frameworks for analyzing causal/treatment effects in randomized experiments.
elements.sent-2411-015: One is the finite population inference \citep{fisher1935design, neyman1923 , also called randomization-based or design-based inference, which uses solely randomization of the treatment assignment as the ``reasoned basis'' and focuses on the units in the experiment.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The other is the usual superpopulation inference that assumes random, e.g., independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.), sampling of units from some population, often with model assumptions on potential outcomes as well as their dependence on covariates.
elements.sent-2411-017: The finite population inference can be more preferable when experiments involve convenient samples, or when the superpopulation is hard to articulate.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, when evaluating certain policy effects on the 50 states of US, it may not be clear what the corresponding superpopulation is.
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, the finite population inference naturally avoids distributional or model assumptions on potential outcomes and covariates.
elements.sent-2411-020: See \citet{Little2004 , \citet{KG2006 , \citet{Rosenbaum02a , \citet{Rosenberger2019 and \citet{Abadie2020 for more comparisons between these two modes of inference.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Superpopulation inference is more flexible for incorporating covariates, often through modeling the conditional distributions of potential outcomes given covariates.
elements.sent-2411-022: It can help conducting efficient inference for treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, such an inference can be sensitive to model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-024: We will conduct a systematic investigation for analyses of randomized experiments that involve statistical models, under the finite population inference allowing arbitrary model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-025: That is, we view the invoked model as a working model rather than a true data-generating model.
elements.sent-2411-026: We will investigate the robust utilization of models for evaluating both average and individual treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-027: To do this, an important first step is to understand large-sample properties of the parameter estimation under randomization.
elements.sent-2411-028: We first establish the theory for M-estimation and Z-estimation under the randomization-based inference framework.
elements.sent-2411-029: In contrast to usual superpopulation inference, the observations can be dependent across units due to the dependence in their treatment assignments, and we may not be able to consistently estimate the asymptotic distributions of the estimators due to the unidentifiable joint distributions of the potential outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-030: We give rigorous conditions for the consistency and asymptotic Normality of the M-estimators and Z-estimators under randomization, and propose asymptotically conservative covariance estimators.
elements.sent-2411-031: We then apply the theory to analyze both average and individual treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-032: We consider three strategies for inferring average treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-033: The model-based approach, which often uses the estimated model parameters, is generally biased.
elements.sent-2411-034: The model-imputed approach, which uses models as a tool for imputing the potential outcomes, is generally biased as well.
elements.sent-2411-035: However, when model parameters are estimated in particular ways, it can provide consistent treatment effect estimation that is robust to any model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, we recommend the model-assisted approach, which uses models to adjust potential outcomes and can always provide consistent treatment effect estimation, and suggest optimal adjustment using generally nonlinear least squares for parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our study on average treatment effects is related to many existing literature.
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, our study for model-based and model-imputed approaches generalizes \citet{Freedman08logistic 's study on logistic regression to general models, and our study for model-imputed and model-assisted approaches intersects with \citet{GuoBasse21 and \citet{Fogarty2020calibration , but with different perspectives.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Our work also shares similar spirits as \citet{Robins2005 , \citet{Rv2010 , \citet{Wooldridge21 , and \citet{VD2002 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, we differ from these by not imposing i.i.d.. assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-041: We further explore how to robustly use models to understand individual treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-042: We propose to directly impose working models for the relationship between individual effects and covariates, despite none of individual effects are observed, and discuss the models' identifiability and inference.
elements.sent-2411-043: The fitted model can be viewed as ``best approximation'' for the distribution of individual effects given covariates.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Potential outcomes, covariates and treatment assignments

elements.sent-2411-056: As discussed in \S \ref{sec:intro , there are two popular inferential frameworks for analyzing randomized experiments: finite population inference and usual superpopulation inference.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Under finite population inference, all the potential outcomes and covariates are viewed as fixed constants (or equivalently being conditioned on), and we use solely the randomization of treatment assignment as the reasoned basis.
elements.sent-2411-058: Under superpopulation inference, some model assumptions are often imposed on the relation between potential outcomes and covariates, and the inference is often driven by classical likelihood theory.
elements.sent-2411-059: In general, if the model is correctly specified, then the model-based approach can provide the most efficient estimation for model parameters, which are often linked to and interpreted as treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-060: However, in practice, the model may be misspecified.
elements.sent-2411-061: Furthermore, even if the model is correctly specified, the targeted model parameter may be different from true causal effects of interest.
elements.sent-2411-062: We will investigate the model-based analysis for randomized experiments under the finite population inference framework, which allows arbitrary model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-063: We focus on the completely randomized experiment (CRE), under which $n_1$ units are randomly assigned to treatment and the remaining $n_0 = N-n_1$ to control.
elements.sent-2411-064: Let $r_1 = n_1/N$ and $r_0 = n_0/N$ be the proportions of treated and control units.
elements.sent-2411-065: We consider estimating a parameter $\theta\in \mathbb{R ^p$ under a CRE, by either minimizing a certain risk function: \begin{equation}\label{eq:M_hat} \hat{M}_N (\bs{\theta}) = \E_N\{ \loss_{Z_i} (Y_i, \bs{X}_i; \bs{\theta}) \} = r_1 \E_{N}^1 \{\loss_1(Y_i, \bs X_i;{\bs\theta})\} + r_0 \E_{N}^0 \{\loss_0(Y_i, \bs X_i;{\bs\theta})\},\end{equation} or finding the root of a certain estimating equation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Z_hat} \hat{\Psi}_N (\bs{\theta}) = \E_N\{ \psi_{Z_i} (Y_i, \bs{X}_i; \bs{\theta}) \} = r_1 \E_{N}^1 \{\psi_1(Y_i, \bs X_i;{\bs\theta})\} + r_0 \E_{N}^0 \{\psi_0(Y_i, \bs X_i;{\bs\theta})\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In \eqref{eq:M_hat and \eqref{eq:Z_hat , $\loss_z$ and $\psi_z$ are prespecified loss and estimating functions taking values in $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{R ^p$, respectively, for observations under treatment arm $z \in \{0, 1\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The estimators from \eqref{eq:M_hat and \eqref{eq:Z_hat are often called the M-estimator and Z-estimator, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We will focus on the Z-estimator in the main paper, since the M-estimator can also be viewed as a Z-estimator by setting $\psi_z = \dot{\loss _z$ for $z=0,1$.}

elements.section-2411-007: Working models for potential outcomes and covariates and maximum likelihood approach
elements.sent-2411-188: We discuss the setup for a general model-based analysis of treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-189: In the following discussion, we will view the models as working models instead of true data-generating models, unless otherwise noted.
elements.sent-2411-190: Typically, we will impose some statistical models on the distribution of potential outcomes given covariates: \begin{equation}\label{eq:model} Y(1)\mid \bs{X} \sim f_1 (y, \bs{X}; \bs{\theta}), \quad Y(0)\mid \bs{X} \sim f_0 (y, \bs{X}; \bs{\theta}),\end{equation} where $f_1(\cdot, \bs{x ; \bs{\theta )$ and $f_0(\cdot, \bs{x ; \bs{\theta )$ denote two generic probability densities that can depend on the covariate vector $\bs{x}$ and some (unknown) parameter vector $\bs{\theta}\in \Theta$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: The experimental units $(Y_i(1), Y_i(0), \bs{X _i)$'s are then assumed to be i.i.d.\ samples from a superpopulation satisfying \eqref{eq:model .}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Here we model both potential outcomes in \eqref{eq:model using the same parameter $\bs{\theta}$, which can allow the two models to share same parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Note that we are considering a CRE where the treatment assignment is unconfounded \citep{pscore1983 .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Hence, the observed outcome for each unit given its covariates follows one of the two potential outcome models in \eqref{eq:model , depending on the treatment assignment, i.e., \begin{equation}Y_i \mid \bs{X}_i, Z_i \sim Z_i \cdot f_1 (y, \bs{X}; \bs{\theta}) + (1-Z_i) \cdot f_0 (y, \bs{X}; \bs{\theta}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-195: We will pay special attention to the class of generalized linear models {\lad with canonical links , which has been widely studied and used \citep[see, e.g.,][]{nelder1989glm, agresti2015foundations .}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Specifically, we assume that the densities of both potential outcomes given covariates belong in a certain exponential dispersion family of the following forms: \begin{equation}\label{eq:glm} Y(z) \mid \bs{x} \sim f_z(y, \bs{x}; \bs{\theta}) = \exp\left\{ \frac{y \cdot (\alpha_z + \bs{\beta}_z^\top \bs{x}) - b_z(\alpha_z + \bs{\beta}_z^\top \bs{x})}{a_z(\bs{\phi}_z)} + c_z(y, \bs{\phi}_z)\right\},\end{equation} where $\bs{\theta = (\alpha_1, \alpha_0, \bs{\beta _1, \bs{\beta _0, \bs{\phi _1, \bs{\phi _0)$ and $z=0,1$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: By the property of the exponential dispersion family, the conditional mean functions of the potential outcomes given covariates under model \eqref{eq:glm are $ h_z(\bs{x ; \bs{\theta ) = \dot b_z ( \alpha_z + \bs{\beta _z^\top \bs{x ) $ for $z=0,1$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-334: In addition, the model-imputed approach using negative binomial regression also provides biased estimation, since the corresponding likelihood equations do not satisfy Condition \ref{cond:consis_MI .}
elements.sent-2411-335: Interestingly, for the negative binomial regression, the model-imputed approach has smaller bias and mean squared error than the model-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-336: Compare the four model-assisted estimators using the same adjustment function but different ways for estimating the parameter, shown in the first, third, sixth and seventh row of Table \ref{tab:simu .}
elements.sent-2411-337: Among them, the model-assisted estimator with squared loss has the smallest root mean squared error and the smallest estimated standard error, confirm its optimality established in \S \ref{sec:optimal_loss_MA .}
elements.sent-2411-338: Moreover, except the one uses Poisson regression without treatment-covariate interaction, these estimators are more precise than those based on linear adjustment functions.
elements.sent-2411-339: This indicates the importance of the form of adjustment functions.
elements.sent-2411-340: We now discuss the three model-assisted estimators linearly adjusting the imputed potential outcomes, shown in the eighth to tenth rows of Table \ref{tab:simu .}
elements.sent-2411-341: All of them improve their original model-assisted estimators in the third, sixth and seventh rows.
elements.sent-2411-342: This is not surprising because (i) we enrich the form of adjustments, and (ii) we estimate the linear adjustment coefficients in the second step using the squared loss that is optimal as studied in \S \ref{sec:optimal_loss_MA .}
elements.sent-2411-343: Under our simulation setting, the improvement is quite small when we use squared loss to estimate the parameter in the imputation function in the first step, whereas it is most substantial we use the maximum likelihood estimator from the Negative binomial model.
elements.sent-2411-344: The coverage probabilities of confidence intervals for the biased estimators are either zero or around $64\%$, which is substantially lower than the nominal level $95\%$.
elements.sent-2411-345: This is not surprising since the biases of these estimator are not negligible compared to their standard deviations.
elements.sent-2411-346: Among the three inconsistent estimators, the model-imputed estimator based on the negative binomial regression model has the smallest bias, which results in the highest coverage probability of about $64\%$ among them.
elements.sent-2411-347: The coverage probabilities of confidence intervals for the consistent estimators are close or above the nominal level, due to the conservativeness in variance estimation.
elements.sent-2411-348: Finally, we try to explain individual effect heterogeneity using covariates.
elements.sent-2411-349: We consider the best linear approximation of the individual effects using either the original covariates or the imputed potential outcomes from the Poisson regression model with treatment-covariate interaction as discussed in Remark \ref{rmk:tran_ind_effect .}

elements.sent-2411-351: Across all simulations, the average values of $R^2$ are, respectively, $0.50$ and $0.71$, when using the original and transformed covariates.
elements.sent-2411-352: These indicate that models can provide useful transformations of original covariates and help better explain treatment effect heterogeneity.

elements.section-2411-014: Uniform convergence and consistency for M-estimation and Z-estimation under simple random sampling
elements.sent-2411-363: In this section, we consider M-estimation and Z-estimation under simple random sampling.
elements.sent-2411-364: We establish the uniform convergence of the empirical risk function and the estimating equation, and the consistency of the M-estimator and Z-estimator.
elements.sent-2411-365: We will consider two types of sufficient conditions, based on either the compactness of the parameter space or the convexity of the risk function.
elements.sent-2411-366: We present the results in the following four subsections.
elements.sent-2411-367: With in each subsection, we first present the two theorems for uniform convergence and consistency, and then present their proofs.

elements.subsection-2411-034: Working model for binary potential outcomes
elements.sent-2411-740: For binary outcomes, individual treatment effects can only take values in $\{-1,0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-741: We can consider the following working model \begin{equation}\label{eq:tau_model_binary} \tau \mid X = \begin{cases} 1 \text{w.p. } \ \exp(x^\top \beta) / \{ \exp(x^\top \beta) + \exp(-x^\top \beta) + \gamma \}, 0 \text{w.p. } \ \gamma / \{ \exp(x^\top \beta) + \exp(-x^\top \beta) + \gamma \}, -1 \text{w.p. } \ \exp(-x^\top \beta) / \{ \exp(x^\top \beta) + \exp(-x^\top \beta) + \gamma \}, \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\gamma$ is a prespecified known constant.
elements.sent-2411-742: The estimation approach in \citet{Tian2014 is equivalent to the maximum likelihood estimation from the working model \eqref{eq:tau_model_binary with $\gamma=2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-743: If we choose $\gamma=1$, then the model \eqref{eq:tau_model_binary reduces to a multinomial logistic regression model.}
elements.sent-2411-744: Intuitively, the model assumes that the the individual effect is more likely to be positive when $x^\top \beta$ is large, and more likely to be negative when $x^\top \beta$ is small.
elements.sent-2411-745: In other words, the individual effect is positively associated with $x^\top \beta$.
elements.sent-2411-746: Under the working model \eqref{eq:tau_model_binary , the probability mass function of $\tau \mid X$ has the following form: \begin{equation}f(\tau \mid x; \beta) = \exp(\tau \cdot x^\top \beta) \cdot \gamma ^ {\mathbb{I}\{\tau = 0\}} / \big[\exp(x^\top \beta) + \exp(-x^\top \beta) + \gamma \big],\end{equation} which falls in the exponential dispersion family in \eqref{eq:model_tau .}}
elements.sent-2411-747: Thus, its parameter is identifiable and can be inferred from the observed data using our Z-estimation theory.
elements.sent-2411-748: If, in addition, we know that the treatment effect is nonnegative for any unit, then the individual treatment effects can only take values in $\{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-749: In this case, we can consider imposing a logistic regression working model on $\tau\mid X$.
elements.sent-2411-750: It also falls in the exponential dispersion family in \eqref{eq:model_tau , and its parameter is identifiable and can be inferred from the observed data.}
